items.author: Eve (Logical Intelligence)
items.content_zh: Logical Intelligence 创始人兼 CEO Eve 做客 Every 的 AI & I，和 Dan Shipper 深度探讨了她押注的 EBM（Energy-Based Models，能量模型）——一种与 LLM 架构完全不同、面向关键任务场景的 AI。她的核心主张是：LLM 本质上是「把一切塞进语言空间来推理」，可当你让它驾驶汽车、设计芯片、平衡电网时，这种硬把空间/工程问题翻译成 token 的做法既昂贵又危险。「想象你坐在一辆由 LLM 驾驶的车里，有人告诉你 20% 的时间它会 hallucinate——你什么感觉？」她拿飞机和自动驾驶反复追问，落点都是同一件事：mission-critical 系统需要的是可验证、token-free、架构本身就自带内部验证器的模型。EBM 把数据映射到能量地形，再用算法在地形里导航；它像鸟瞰而非 LLM 的左脑——「LLM 看到路上有洞，它也会掉进去，因为它是 autoregressive，回不了头」。Eve 判断 LLM 进展正在 plateau：参数、算力、并行推理都堆到极致，下一次相变看不到。更关键的是 B2B 缺口——银行、交易、药物研发、电网，这些行业今天几乎零 LLM 自动化，因为数据不能外送、需要内部验证、对延迟和正确性有硬约束。她也透露了一个信号：部分头部大厂已经在内部做 EBM，对 Logical Intelligence 是正向确认。
items.content_en: Logical Intelligence founder/CEO Eve joins Dan Shipper on AI & I for a sharp case for Energy-Based Models (EBMs) as the architecture built for what LLMs structurally can't do. Her core thesis: LLMs force every problem into language space, which gets expensive and dangerous once you use them to drive cars, design chips, or balance an energy grid. 'Imagine you're in a car driven by an LLM and someone tells you 20% of the time it's gonna hallucinate — how would you feel?' She argues mission-critical systems need verifiable, token-free models whose architecture allows internal verification, not LLMs with external Lean4-style verifiers bolted on. EBMs map data onto an energy landscape and navigate it algorithmically — 'a bird's-eye view,' versus the LLM's autoregressive left brain that 'falls into the hole in the road because it can't turn back.' Eve thinks LLM progress is plateauing ('incremental better, no next phase transition') and points to a huge unserved B2B gap — banks, trading firms, drug discovery, grid operations — where zero data analysis is automated by LLMs because of privacy, verification, and latency constraints. She also drops a tell: some top model companies are now building EBMs in-house, which she reads as validation.

items.author: Amjad Masad
items.content_zh: Replit CEO Amjad Masad 抛出一个新指标思路：如果 Mythos 这类前沿模型能把漏洞挖掘完全自动化，那 GitHub 该给每个仓库加一个「投入在加固上的算力美元值」，摆在 star 数旁边。他举例：`linus/linux ⭐ 200k 🦾 $239M`，认为这是「开源能被信任的唯一出路」。背后逻辑是：AI 自动化让攻击面扩大到所有开源依赖，未来开源的信任不再来自「谁写的」「多少人用」，而来自「被安全算力盯过多久」。
items.content_en: Replit CEO Amjad Masad floats a new metric: if frontier models like Mythos can fully automate vulnerability discovery, GitHub should display a 'compute-dollars spent hardening this package' number next to stars. His example — `linus/linux ⭐ 200k 🦾 $239M` — and his claim is that this is 'the only way OSS can be trusted.' The implicit thesis: as AI expands the attack surface to every open-source dependency, trust stops being about author reputation or usage count and starts being about how much security compute has been pointed at the repo.

items.author: Guillermo Rauch
items.content_zh: Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch 放大了 basement.studio 的 Shader Lab 案例，给出一个更大的论断：未来的设计不是单一工具，而是每个团队都有自己的「design factory」。Shader Lab 用 Claude Code + three.js + Next.js + Vercel 按 basement 的特定需求一次性造出来——他把这条路径总结为「the software factory is the product」。Rauch 的暗示很直接：当团队能够按需为单一项目构建专属工具时，标准化 SaaS 工具的溢价空间会持续被挤压，而有造厂能力的小团队会先吃到红利。
items.content_en: Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch boosts basement.studio's Shader Lab and pushes a bigger frame: the future of design isn't one tool — it's every team having their own 'design factory.' Shader Lab was built with Claude Code, three.js, Next.js, and Vercel to basement's exact spec, and Rauch summarizes the shift as 'the software factory is the product.' His implied thesis: once small teams can spin up bespoke internal tools per project on demand, the premium on standardized SaaS tools compresses, and teams with factory capability eat first.

items.author: Peter Yang
items.bio: Product at Roblox; AI tutorial creator
items.content_zh: Roblox 产品负责人、AI 教程博主 Peter Yang 刚结束两周的中国行，列出 15 条给产品人的一手观察。他的立场鲜明：每个 product builder 都该去一次中国，亲眼看看「世界第二大经济体到底怎么运转」——AI 工作文化、电车、2 美元外卖，以及当地人的真实生活与工作节奏。他没把这当成旅游笔记，而是作为「理解 AI 时代下一个增量市场」的尽调素材。
items.content_en: Roblox product lead and AI tutorial creator Peter Yang returns from a two-week China trip and publishes 15 observations aimed squarely at product builders. His argument is blunt: every product builder should visit at least once to actually see how 'the world's second-largest economy operates' — AI work culture, EVs, $2 delivery, and how people live and work day to day. He's not framing this as travel content; he's framing it as due diligence on the next incremental market for AI products.

items.author: Peter Steinberger
items.content_zh: OpenClaw 负责人 Peter Steinberger 发出一条带刺的警告：看看 GPT-5.4-Cyber 在闭源逆向上的能力，「有坏消息告诉你」。他顺带点名了整个开源 agent 生态——上百个团队天天尝试攻破 OpenClaw，他们的回应是快速迭代和 code hardening，哪怕偶尔引入 regression 也要推进；而那些「忽视安全工作、不公开安全设计」的其他开源项目/harness，在他看来才是真正的风险。潜台词：当 AI 自动化攻击门槛骤降，「没做过安全概念」的 agent harness 事实上已经不安全。
items.content_en: OpenClaw lead Peter Steinberger throws a barbed warning: look at GPT-5.4-Cyber's closed-source reverse-engineering ability — 'I have bad news for you.' He reframes OpenClaw's four months of painful security work as the only defensible path: hundreds of teams are constantly probing it, OpenClaw responds with rapid iteration and hardening (accepting occasional regressions), while 'other open source projects/harnesses that ignore this work and don't publish their security design' are the real danger. The subtext is sharp: once AI collapses the cost of attack, any agent harness without an explicit security concept is effectively insecure.

items.author: Aditya Agarwal
items.bio: South Park Commons GP; Bevel Health co-founder
items.content_zh: South Park Commons GP、前 Dropbox CTO Aditya Agarwal 总结了一条「LLM 怀疑者的演化曲线」：1) 只是自动补全；2) 只是 next-token 预测；3) 会 hallucinate；4) 只能处理训练数据；5) 只对写代码有用；6) 只对可验证任务有用。他的落点是「承认吧，LLM 就是很厉害」——与其说是嘲讽，不如说是在提醒 builder：每一个「只能用于 X」的防线被突破，都意味着一个新的可交付产品品类打开。
items.content_en: South Park Commons GP and former Dropbox CTO Aditya Agarwal maps the LLM skeptic's arc: 1) they're just autocorrect; 2) just next-token predictors; 3) they hallucinate; 4) useful only for training-set data; 5) only good for coding; 6) only good for verifiable things. His punchline — 'Just admit it! LLMs are awesome' — reads less as a dunk and more as a reminder to builders: every 'only good for X' line that falls opens a fresh product category that was previously considered unreachable.
